findings.dominantFinding: The dominant imaging finding is a focal enhancing lesion situated entirely within the spinal cord parenchyma, causing focal cord expansion [1]. There is typically a discrete enhancing nidus surrounded by a disproportionately large area of T2-hyperintense vasogenic edema extending longitudinally [1].
findings.supportingFeatures: Supporting features include prominent enhancement following gadolinium administration [1, 2]. Additionally, there may be leptomeningeal enhancement representing pial metastatic lesions, or concurrent osseous metastases in the adjacent vertebral bodies to suggest a disseminated malignant process [1].
findings.criticalNegatives: Importantly, there is no evidence of prominent serpentine flow voids or a macroscopic cyst with an intensely enhancing mural nodule to suggest a hemangioblastoma [1, 3]. Furthermore, there is no central canal dilatation or prominent benign intratumoral cysts, which are classically associated with primary cord ependymomas [2].
findings.influentialFeature: The feature that most influences my thinking is the presence of an enhancing intramedullary nodule with a disproportionately extensive region of surrounding cord edema relative to the size of the lesion [1].

differentials.primaryDiagnosis: The appearances are most in keeping with Metastatic disease (breast cancer), given the focal intramedullary enhancing mass with extensive surrounding edema [1]. Intramedullary metastases are relatively uncommon compared to other spinal compartments, but when present, they classically originate from lung or breast primaries and incite profound cord edema [1].
differentials.principleDifferential: The principle differential would be Ependymoma, however this is a primary spinal cord tumor that typically presents as a centrally located, circumscribed mass spanning multiple segments with associated intratumoral cysts [2]. Furthermore, an ependymoma does not typically evoke the massive, disproportionate surrounding edema seen with acute metastatic seeding [1, 2].
differentials.excludeDiagnosis: A diagnosis I would want to exclude is Astrocytoma, given this infiltrating primary cord tumor also causes diffuse cord expansion, and missing it could alter primary neuro-oncological management [1]. However, astrocytomas typically present as infiltrating, expansile masses rather than a discrete enhancing nidus with massive edema, and they are the most common spinal tumors in children [1].

management.priority: From a radiological perspective, the priority is to rapidly assess the degree of spinal cord swelling to guide urgent administration of corticosteroids and prevent irreversible neurological deficit.
management.priorImaging: I would review any available prior imaging to assess disease behaviour — whether this is stable, progressive, or improving. Comparison for interval change is crucial to establish if the patient has a known history of advanced breast carcinoma and to evaluate the response to any prior systemic chemotherapy or radiotherapy.
management.furtherImaging: I would recommend a contrast-enhanced MRI of the entire neuroaxis (brain and whole spine), as this modality is superior here for evaluating the entire neuroaxis for further asymptomatic drop metastases, leptomeningeal spread, and intracranial metastatic disease [4].
management.mriSequences: I would specify sagittal and axial T1-weighted pre- and post-contrast sequences to definitively localise the enhancing nidus within the cord parenchyma, alongside T2-weighted and STIR sequences to accurately map the true longitudinal extent of the associated cord edema [1, 2].
management.intervention: If the patient does not have a known primary malignancy, I would specify an ultrasound-guided core biopsy of a more accessible breast or extracranial metastatic lesion rather than attempting a high-risk intramedullary cord biopsy.
management.mdtDiscussion: This should go to the Neuro-oncology MDT. My report would clearly document the exact level of the intramedullary metastasis, the longitudinal extent of the associated cord edema, and any impending cord compromise to expedite urgent radiotherapy and high-dose steroid treatment.

vivaAnswer.fullScript:
The dominant imaging finding is a focal enhancing lesion situated entirely within the spinal cord parenchyma, causing focal cord expansion [1]. There is typically a discrete enhancing nidus surrounded by a disproportionately large area of T2-hyperintense vasogenic edema extending longitudinally [1]. Supporting features include prominent enhancement following gadolinium administration [1, 2]. Additionally, there may be leptomeningeal enhancement representing pial metastatic lesions, or concurrent osseous metastases in the adjacent vertebral bodies to suggest a disseminated malignant process [1]. Importantly, there is no evidence of prominent serpentine flow voids or a macroscopic cyst with an intensely enhancing mural nodule to suggest a hemangioblastoma [1, 3]. Furthermore, there is no central canal dilatation or prominent benign intratumoral cysts, which are classically associated with primary cord ependymomas [2]. The feature that most influences my thinking is the presence of an enhancing intramedullary nodule with a disproportionately extensive region of surrounding cord edema relative to the size of the lesion [1].

The appearances are most in keeping with Metastatic disease (breast cancer), given the focal intramedullary enhancing mass with extensive surrounding edema [1]. Intramedullary metastases are relatively uncommon compared to other spinal compartments, but when present, they classically originate from lung or breast primaries and incite profound cord edema [1]. The principle differential would be Ependymoma, however this is a primary spinal cord tumor that typically presents as a centrally located, circumscribed mass spanning multiple segments with associated intratumoral cysts [2]. Furthermore, an ependymoma does not typically evoke the massive, disproportionate surrounding edema seen with acute metastatic seeding [1, 2]. A diagnosis I would want to exclude is Astrocytoma, given this infiltrating primary cord tumor also causes diffuse cord expansion, and missing it could alter primary neuro-oncological management [1]. However, astrocytomas typically present as infiltrating, expansile masses rather than a discrete enhancing nidus with massive edema, and they are the most common spinal tumors in children [1].

From a radiological perspective, the priority is to rapidly assess the degree of spinal cord swelling to guide urgent administration of corticosteroids and prevent irreversible neurological deficit. I would review any available prior imaging to assess disease behaviour — whether this is stable, progressive, or improving. Comparison for interval change is crucial to establish if the patient has a known history of advanced breast carcinoma and to evaluate the response to any prior systemic chemotherapy or radiotherapy. I would recommend a contrast-enhanced MRI of the entire neuroaxis (brain and whole spine), as this modality is superior here for evaluating the entire neuroaxis for further asymptomatic drop metastases, leptomeningeal spread, and intracranial metastatic disease [4]. I would specify sagittal and axial T1-weighted pre- and post-contrast sequences to definitively localise the enhancing nidus within the cord parenchyma, alongside T2-weighted and STIR sequences to accurately map the true longitudinal extent of the associated cord edema [1, 2]. If the patient does not have a known primary malignancy, I would specify an ultrasound-guided core biopsy of a more accessible breast or extracranial metastatic lesion rather than attempting a high-risk intramedullary cord biopsy. This should go to the Neuro-oncology MDT. My report would clearly document the exact level of the intramedullary metastasis, the longitudinal extent of the associated cord edema, and any impending cord compromise to expedite urgent radiotherapy and high-dose steroid treatment.
